runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin uncompensated annual-hours elasticity of labor supply for prime-age workers in the United States who are already working
citations: Pencavel (1986) on labor supply elasticities
Blundell and MaCurdy (1999) survey of empirical labor supply
Chetty et al. (2012) on bounds to elasticities
runs.reasoningSummary: The uncompensated elasticity combines substitution effects (positive, workers increase hours when wages rise) and income effects (negative, higher wages reduce work incentives). For prime-age employed workers, the literature suggests modest positive intensive-margin elasticities, typically in the range of 0.1–0.3. The income effect partially offsets the substitution effect for this demographic. My central estimate reflects the consensus that intensive-margin responses are relatively inelastic but non-trivial, with substantial uncertainty about the true magnitude across different identification strategies and contexts.

runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin uncompensated annual-hours elasticity of labor supply for prime-age working individuals in the United States, reflecting the percent change in annual hours worked from a 1 percent change in net-of-tax wage.
citations: Pencavel (1986) - longitudinal study finding modest male labor supply elasticity around 0.1
Killingsworth and Heckman (1986) - comprehensive review summarizing elasticities typically between 0 and 0.2
Blundell and MaCurdy (1999) - structural labor supply estimates suggesting uncompensated elasticities in range of 0.1-0.2 for men
runs.reasoningSummary: Empirical literature on intensive-margin labor supply for prime-age workers consistently finds modest uncompensated wage elasticities. For men, estimates typically cluster around 0.1-0.2. For women, estimates are somewhat higher but still moderate. The uncompensated elasticity combines substitution and income effects; income effects are often negative (higher wages → reduced hours) but smaller than substitution effects for most workers. Central estimate of 0.15 reflects consensus that elasticities are small but meaningfully positive, with substantial uncertainty acknowledged.

runs.reasoningSummary: The uncompensated elasticity combines positive substitution effects (work more when wages rise) with negative income effects (work less when richer).
